perturbation.original_text: The Company hereby warrants that all Products sold to the Customer hereunder, at the time of shipment to the Customer, (a) will be merchantable and of generally commercially salable quality; and (b) will have a then remaining shelf life of at least twelve (12) months. The Company further 8 -7- warrants that the Products have been manufactured, labeled and packaged, and when in the Company's possession or under its control, have been handled, stored and shipped, in compliance with all applicable federal, state and local laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company hereby warrants that all Products sold to the Customer hereunder, at the time of shipment to the Customer, (a) will be merchantable and of generally commercially salable quality; and (b) will have a then remaining shelf life of at least twelve (12) months. The Company further 8 -7- warrants that the Products have been manufactured, labeled and packaged.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'and when in the Company's possession or under its control, have been handled, stored and shipped, in compliance with all applicable federal, state and local laws' from its original warranty section introduces uncertainty about the company's adherence to laws during handling and storage, affecting enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 351 - Adulterated drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the warranty regarding compliance with applicable laws during handling and storage, the contract creates uncertainty and potentially contradicts the requirements of 21 U.S. Code § 351, which prohibits the introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any drug or device that has been adulterated. The modification makes it harder to enforce the company's responsibility for ensuring the products are not adulterated due to improper handling or storage.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase regarding compliance with applicable laws during handling, storage, and shipping directly contradicts the principle outlined in 21 U.S. Code § 351, which focuses on preventing the adulteration of drugs and devices. By removing the company's warranty to follow all applicable laws during these processes, the contract introduces uncertainty about whether the products will be handled in a manner that prevents adulteration, a core concern of the law. As shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 351 explicitly addresses adulterated drugs and devices, including those prepared or held under insanitary conditions. The modified contract text weakens the enforceability of ensuring the company's adherence to sanitary and lawful handling, storage, and shipment procedures, thus contradicting the law's intention.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, if either the Company or the Agent is delayed in or prevented from fulfilling any of its obligations hereunder by reason of any cause beyond its reasonable control (including, but not limited to acts of God, fire, third party strike, flood, delay of transportation or inability to obtain necessary raw materials through normal commercial channels), then that party will not be liable under this Agreement for damages resulting from such delay or failure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, if either the Company or the Agent is delayed in or prevented from fulfilling any of its obligations hereunder by reason of any cause beyond its reasonable control (including, but not limited to acts of God, fire, third party strike, flood). then that party will not be liable under this Agreement for damages resulting from such delay or failure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'delay of transportation or inability to obtain necessary raw materials through normal commercial channels' introduces contradiction—it limits the scope of force majeure events. This restructuring of the sentence introduces uncertainty regarding typical force majeure events, and thus reduces clarity and potentially enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCC § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing specific examples of force majeure events typically covered under UCC § 2-615 (such as inability to obtain necessary raw materials), the modified clause narrows the scope of excusable delays, potentially contradicting the intent and application of the UCC's force majeure provisions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the scope of force majeure events by removing specific examples like 'delay of transportation or inability to obtain necessary raw materials through normal commercial channels.' According to the law explanation, this could contradict the intent and application of UCC § 2-615's force majeure provisions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-615, addresses excuses for failure of presupposed conditions, stating that 'Delay in delivery or non-delivery...is not a breach of his duty...if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made.' The original text's inclusion of specific examples provided clarity regarding what constitutes a force majeure event, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting the intent of UCC § 2-615 by limiting the events that excuse performance.
